titleStmt.title: Letter from John Chamberlain to Dudley Carleton, 22 November 1598

publicationStmt.p: Published digitally at earlymodernletters.org. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

div.p: This longe and permanent abode of westerly windes bringes us no newes but such as we least desire to heare of — I meane out of Ireland, whence messengers come dayly (like Jobs servants) laden with yll tidings of new troubles and revokes. The English of Mounster are much blamed for making no better proofe of themselves, and geving so easie way to the rebells; and (as goode I telle yt you as another) Sir Thomas NorrisSir Thomas Norris (1556–1599), Lord President of Munster from 1597. Chamberlain's report that he took the alarm too soon and abandoned his station reflects the widespread English criticism of the Munster commanders after the collapse of the plantation in autumn 1598. Norris died in July 1599, worn out by the Irish command. hath his part with the rest and is thought to have taken the alarme too soone and left his station before there was neede, whereby the ennemie was too much incouraged, and those that were well affected or stoode indifferent forced to follow the tide. The remedies of these mischeifes go as slowly forward as other matters, only it is saide the erle of Essex shall go thether about February or March with as ample commission as ever any had, the conditions wherof were lost labour to set downe because they varie and alter every weeke, and withall his going is not so fully resolved but that once in ten dayes yt is in question.

The day that we looked for Stanlies arraignment he came not himself but sent his forerunner one Squire that had ben an under purvayer of the stable, who being in Spaine was delt withall by one WalpooleFather Richard Walpole SJ (c. 1564–1607), an English Jesuit working in Spain; brother of the martyr Henry Walpole (executed 1595). It was Walpole who recruited Squire and provided the poisonous preparation. Walpole later denied the whole affair. a Jesuite, to poison the Quene and the earle of Essex, and accordingly came prepared into England, and went with the earle in his owne ship the last jorny,The Islands Voyage of 1597, Essex's expedition to the Azores. Squire sailed in Essex's own ship, which gave him access to the chair where Essex sat. and poisoned the armes or handles of the chaire he used to sit in with a confection he had receved of the Jesuite, as likewise he had don the pommell of the Quenes saddle not past five dayes before his going to sea: but because nothing succeded of yt, the priest thincking he had either chaunged his purpose or bewraied yt, gave Stanly instructions to accuse him, thereby to get him more credit and to be revenged of Squire for breaking promise. The fellow seemed very penitent and confest the whole practise. The Quenes atturneyEdward Coke (1552–1634), Attorney General from 1594; later Chief Justice of Common Pleas (1606) and of King's Bench (1613), and one of the great figures of English common law. The surprise of this marriage — Chamberlain notes it with characteristic scepticism — lay in Lady Hatton's choosing a man of lesser social standing over the many "large and likely offers" she had received. the seventh of this month married the Lady HattonElizabeth Cecil (1578–1646), daughter of Thomas Cecil, second Lord Burghley (later first Earl of Exeter); widow of Sir William Hatton (d. 1597), nephew and heir of the great Christopher Hatton. As Lady Hatton she became one of the leading hostesses of the Jacobean court, and her marriage to Coke was notoriously turbulent. to the great admiration of all men, that after so many large and likely offers she shold decline to a man of his qualitie, and the world will not beleve that yt was without a misterie. The day before, Sir John PackingtonSir John Packington (1549–1625), Worcestershire knight, knighted by Essex in 1596; known as "Lusty Packington" at court. He married Dorothy Smith, widow of Benedict Barnham, a London mercer. maried Mistris BarnhamDorothy Smith (dates unknown), daughter of Humphrey Smith, silkman to Queen Elizabeth, and widow of Benedict Barnham, a liveryman of the Drapers' Company., one of our London widowes. The erle of Southampton is come home and for his welcome committed to the Fleet,Southampton had returned from France; his confinement in the Fleet prison followed from the Queen's continued displeasure at his secret marriage to Elizabeth Vernon. Chamberlain adds that he was already rumoured to be on the point of release. but I heare he is already upon his delivery.

The Quene came to Whitehall the last weeke, being receved a mile out of towne by the Lord Mayor and his bretheren accompanied with 400 velvet cotes and chaines of gold. Her days passed without any extraordinarie matter more then running and ringing.Running at the ring and bell-ringing were customary celebrations for Accession Day (17 November), the anniversary of Elizabeth's accession in 1558; the Queen arrived at Whitehall on 14 November, three days before the annual festivities. Your brother Carleton hath ben in towne most part of this terme but is now gon. Other newes we have none so that with all my best wishes I bid you farewell.

postscript.p: To my very goode frend Master Dudley Carleton attending on the Lord Governor of Ostend geve these.
